key_evidence: ITUC 2026 Global Rights Index: Bangladesh exited the 10 worst countries for workers' rights for the first time since 2017, driven by November 2025 labour-law reforms.

Reforms eased union formation (20 workers vs prior 20% threshold), added 120-day maternity leave, anti-discrimination protections, right to refuse unsafe work, and extended coverage to domestic/agricultural/shipbreaking workers.
Bangladesh ratified ILO Conventions C155 (OSH), C187 and C190 (violence and harassment) and highlighted progress at the 114th ILC in Geneva, June 2026.
Gains tempered: ITUC retained Rating 5 ('no guarantee of rights'); SEZ workers remain excluded from union rights; employer resistance and a 2025 tear-gas dispersal persist.

evidence.quote: moved out of the list of the world's 10 worst countries for workers' rights in 2026 for the first time since 2017, although the country remained in rating 5, 'no guarantee of rights'
evidence.claim: Bangladesh exited the ITUC 10-worst list in 2026 but retained the lowest rights rating
evidence.source: New Age (reporting ITUC Global Rights Index 2026)

evidence.quote: unions to be established with the support of as few as 20 workers, replacing the previous requirement for approval from 20 per cent of a company's total workforce
evidence.claim: November 2025 reform substantially eased trade-union formation thresholds

evidence.quote: freedom of association remained heavily restricted because of regulatory barriers and strong resistance from employers
evidence.claim: Significant freedom-of-association gaps persist despite the reforms, capping the upgrade
evidence.source: ITUC Global Rights Index 2026 via New Age
